topic_summary: Recitation on academic promotion and tenure at MIT, prompted by student questions about academic careers and (separately) about the Jeffrey Epstein / MIT Media Lab affair. Tom explains the four faculty ranks (AP, AWOT, associate with tenure, full professor), the tenure timeline, the role of external letters, the Science Citation Index as crutch, contrasts the 1960s fiefdom system with present-day Career Development Committees, and tells personal stories: being labeled as not making tenure threshold ten months after hire (1977 letter from Oregon Graduate Center), Bob Mehrabian's refusal to switch from solidification to welding, Marg the secretary who was kept on salary while caring for her mother in Oklahoma, and Tom's polluted-tenure-case confrontation with Flemings, Bob Brown, and Chuck Vest. Closes with a money-or-power framing of institutional behavior connecting back to the Epstein question.
people_named: Jeffrey Epstein (referenced, not named in detail)
key_dates_mentioned: May 1977 (Oregon letter declaring Tom won't make tenure threshold)
1983 (Flemings becomes dept head, institutes Career Development Committees)
1989 (Tom acting dept head while Flemings on sabbatical)
1995 (Tom becomes dept head; Flemings steps down January 15)
